\needspace{4\baselineskip}
\section{Mai Tai}
\recipemeta{Double Old-Fashioned · Rum · Garnish: spent lime shell and mint sprig}
\recipedescription{Trader Vic's 1944 rum sour with orgeat and orange curaçao, structurally a refined Jamaican planter's-punch sour built around a specific lost Jamaican rum.}
\subsection*{Ingredients}
\begin{ingredients}
\item \textbf{fresh lime juice} -- 1 oz
\item \textbf{rock candy syrup} -- 0.25 oz
\item \textbf{orgeat} -- 0.25 oz
\item \textbf{orange curacao} -- 0.5 oz
\item \textbf{aged Martinique agricole} -- 1 oz
\item \textbf{aged pot-still Jamaican rum} -- 1 oz
\item \textbf{crushed ice} -- 1 cup
\item \textbf{lime shell} -- 1
\item \textbf{mint sprig} -- 1
\end{ingredients}
\subsection*{Instructions}
\note{Vic claimed he invented the drink at his Oakland bar in 1944 and christened it after his Tahitian friend Carrie Guild took a sip and exclaimed "Mai Tai-Roa Aé" — "out of this world, the best."\textsuperscript{1} Donn Beach later asserted he had served a Mai Tai at his Hollywood Don the Beachcomber's as early as 1933, and the two men feuded publicly over authorship; modern historiography generally credits Vic with the drink as it is known today (the rum-orgeat-curaçao-lime sour), while acknowledging Donn's earlier and structurally unrelated namesake.\textsuperscript{2} Vic's 1944 spec called for a single 17-year J. Wray \& Nephew Jamaican rum;\textsuperscript{3} when that stock dried up in the 1950s Vic himself reformulated with a blend of Jamaican and Martinique rums, and modern reconstructions follow that split-base approach to approximate the lost rum's profile.\textsuperscript{4} The Mai Tai's structural bones are the Jamaican planter's-punch sour tradition (one of sour, two of sweet, three of strong, four of weak) — it descends from West Indian punch, not Polynesian invention.\textsuperscript{5} \par Brand recommendations. Best: Smith \& Cross or Hamilton Pot Still Black for the Jamaican; Rhum J.M. VSOP or Clément VSOP for the agricole; Pierre Ferrand Dry Curaçao; Small Hand Foods or BG Reynolds orgeat. Economical: Appleton Estate 8 + Plantation 3 Stars (or Denizen Merchant's Reserve as a single-bottle stand-in); Cointreau; Liber \& Co. orgeat. Single-bottle alternative: Denizen Merchant's Reserve, an 8-year blend of Plummer-style Jamaican pot-still rum and Martinique rhum grand arôme. It was developed by Nick Pelis with Martin Cate of Smuggler's Cove specifically to fill the slot of the lost J. Wray \& Nephew 17, approximating the blend Vic himself reached for in the "second adjusted" Mai Tai recipe after the original Jamaican rum stock dried up. Pour 2 oz of Merchant's Reserve in place of the split rum bill.}
\begin{enumerate}
\item In a shaker combine fresh lime juice, rock candy syrup, orgeat, orange curacao, aged Martinique agricole, and aged pot-still Jamaican rum.
\item Add crushed ice and shake until chilled, about 10 seconds.
\item Pour unstrained into a double old-fashioned glass.
\item Garnish with the spent lime shell and a mint sprig.
\end{enumerate}
\note{\textbf{Sources.} \par \textsuperscript{1} Bergeron, \textit{Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide, Revised} (1972), pp. 163–164 — Vic's own creation narrative, including the "Mai Tai-Roa Aé" christening by Carrie Guild and the closing line "Anybody who says I didn't create this drink is a dirty stinker." Vic was self-promotional and the narrative should be read as advocacy, not neutral history; cited here for the christening anecdote, which is corroborated nowhere else but uncontested. \par \textsuperscript{2} Berry, \textit{Sippin' Safari} (2017 ed.), Mai Tai chapter; Cate \& Cate, \textit{Smuggler's Cove} (2016), Mai Tai entry (p. 261; history pp. 262–64) — both document the Vic / Donn Beach authorship dispute and conclude that the modern Mai Tai (rum-orgeat-curaçao-lime sour) is Vic's, while Donn's earlier "Mai Tai" was a different drink that shared only the name. \par \textsuperscript{3} Berry, \textit{Sippin' Safari} (2017 ed.), Mai Tai chapter — documents the 17-year J. Wray \& Nephew Jamaican rum as the original 1944 base and traces the rum's disappearance from the market. \par \textsuperscript{4} Cate \& Cate, \textit{Smuggler's Cove} (2016), Mai Tai entry (p. 261) — describes the modern split-base reconstruction (aged Jamaican + aged Martinique agricole) and the Denizen Merchant's Reserve collaboration with Nick Pelis to recreate the lost J. Wray \& Nephew profile in a single bottle. \par \textsuperscript{5} Curtis, \textit{And a Bottle of Rum} (2006), planter's-punch chapter — traces the West Indian planter's-punch tradition ("one of sour, two of sweet, three of strong, four of weak") that underlies the Mai Tai's structural template. \par \textsuperscript{6} Beachbum Berry, "Mai Tai." https://beachbumberry.com/recipe-mai-tai.html (accessed 2026-05-03) — Berry's published reconstruction of the 1944 spec, the source for this file's measurement bill.}
\recipeattribution{Recipe by Victor "Trader Vic" Bergeron (1944, Trader Vic's, Oakland)}
\vspace{3\baselineskip}
